Q1: I tried to keep a pretty consistent study routine, honestly. I'd usually set aside time in the evenings after my extracurricular activities to go through the course materials. Being a visual learner, I'd spend a lot of time rewatching lecture videos and making diagrams or color-coded notes. I aimed for about 18 hours a week spread across most days, though I'll admit it didn't always translate into exam success like I hoped it would.
Q2: I really tried to stick to regular times, mostly because my anxiety gets worse when things feel chaotic. But yeah, when deadlines piled up or when I had commitments with my extracurriculars, everything would shift around. I'd end up cramming more into certain days and then feeling exhausted. The inconsistency stressed me out even though I was technically still putting in the hours.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was an assignment coming up or when I got a notification about new content. Sometimes my peers from the peer support group would message me about something posted, and that would prompt me to check. I didn't log in super frequently—maybe five times a week or so—but when I did, I tried to make it count by watching videos and checking discussions.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. It never felt automatic for me. I'd have to consciously remind myself to log in, and sometimes I'd forget for days. The peer support helped because my study buddies would check in, and that external push made me more likely to access the LMS. But on my own, it was always a deliberate effort rather than a habit.
Q5: The lecture videos were definitely the most useful for me because I'm a visual learner. I spent a lot of time—almost 195 minutes—watching and rewatching them to understand concepts. The discussion posts were also helpful because interacting with peers helped clarify things I was confused about. Seeing how other students approached problems gave me different perspectives that I wouldn't have figured out alone.
Q6: I often skipped the reading materials, especially dense PDFs or text-heavy resources. My brain just doesn't absorb information well that way—I need visuals, diagrams, something more engaging. I'd also delay looking at feedback sometimes because my anxiety made me nervous about what I'd see. It's not that I didn't care; I just had to mentally prepare myself first.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see it directly connecting to assignments or when it helped me visualize a concept. If I watched a video and suddenly understood something that had been confusing me, that felt valuable. But if something felt like busywork or didn't seem to connect to what we'd be assessed on, I'd question whether it was worth the effort given how limited my resources and time were.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't really afford. With low resources and high anxiety about my grades, I focused almost entirely on required work. Required activities had this weight to them—I knew they counted toward my grade, so I'd prioritize them even when I was exhausted. Optional stuff felt nice in theory but practically impossible to get to.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a ton of materials and studied them offline because I don't always have reliable internet access at home. I'd screenshot diagrams, save videos when I could, and work from printed notes. I also used external YouTube videos and free resources online to supplement what the course offered. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying intensely from my downloaded notes or working through problems on paper, and none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd spend hours redrawing diagrams from memory or explaining concepts to my peer support group in person. The LMS only sees when I'm logged in, not when I'm at my desk with my notebook at 2 AM trying to understand something.
Q11: Yeah, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video or PDF once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never need to open it again in the LMS. My login frequency was low, but that doesn't mean I wasn't engaging with the content. I just preferred having everything saved locally so I could study without worrying about internet issues.
Q12: Around exam time, things got really hard. My anxiety spiked, and even though I'd done well on assignments, I couldn't translate that into exam performance. I'd freeze up, second-guess everything I knew. There were also weeks when my extracurricular commitments were intense, and I just couldn't give the course the attention it needed. My resilience isn't great, so setbacks hit me hard.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just kind of stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars and the stress of having limited financial resources made everything harder. Sometimes I'd have to choose between buying materials for an activity or printing notes for class—those decisions weighed on me.
Q14: Yeah, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, maybe a video playing, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about exams, thinking about money stuff, or just too anxious to focus. I'd technically be 'engaged' according to the system, but I wasn't actually absorbing anything. It's frustrating because the time was spent but not productively.
Q15: I postponed looking at my exam results and feedback a lot. My anxiety made it really hard to face potentially bad news. I also avoided starting certain assignments until the last minute because the pressure of doing them perfectly paralyzed me. I knew procrastinating made things worse, but knowing that didn't make it easier to stop. It's like my brain was protecting me from stress by creating more stress.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage. I'd log in more frequently as due dates approached, cramming in video watching and discussion posts. My task completion was high because I refused to miss deadlines, but the quality of my engagement definitely suffered when I was rushing. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have logged in much at all honestly.
Q17: Feedback was a double-edged sword for me. When it was positive, like on my assignments where I scored really well, it motivated me to keep going. But I'd often delay reading feedback because I was scared it would be negative. Once I finally looked at it, it did help me understand what to improve, but the anxiety barrier meant there was always a delay between receiving and actually using feedback.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but having four online courses simultaneously was overwhelming. I appreciated that materials were organized visually in the LMS because that matched my learning style. But the pacing sometimes felt off—too much content released at once, or not enough time between heavy topics. The structure didn't account for students like me who need more time to process things.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because I could pause, rewind, and rewatch until things clicked visually. But the heavy reliance on text-based readings made things harder for me. Also, the exam format didn't match how I learned—I could do assignments well because I had time to think and create visual representations, but timed exams triggered my anxiety and didn't let me show what I actually knew.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me, actually. I watched tons of videos and participated in discussions, but my exam score was terrible. High activity might mean someone is trying really hard but struggling with test anxiety, or they're engaging with content in ways that don't translate to the specific assessment format. It doesn't necessarily mean they're not learning—maybe the assessments just don't capture their understanding.
Q21: That's kind of the opposite of my situation, but I can see how it happens. Some people are just efficient—they learn quickly from minimal exposure, or they have strong background knowledge. Or maybe they're studying heavily offline like I do, so their LMS activity looks low even though they're actually working hard. The system can't see everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, how long videos play. But they miss so much. They can't see me studying from printed notes, discussing concepts with my peer support group in person, or lying awake at night mentally reviewing material. They definitely can't measure understanding or anxiety levels. A log shows behavior, not learning or emotional state.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, connecting ideas—even if I'm doing that while staring at my ceiling instead of at a screen. For me, engagement also means reaching out to peers when I'm stuck and being willing to struggle through confusion rather than giving up. It's mental investment, not just clicks.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected everything—the hours I spent worrying instead of studying, the mental energy it took just to open my grades. It wouldn't show my peer support group meetings where we worked through problems together in person, or how I taught myself concepts using YouTube videos and hand-drawn diagrams. It also wouldn't show that despite failing, I genuinely tried and learned things that just didn't show up on the exam.
